title: Laghu Ajitaśānti Stava (the Short Ajitaśānti Hymn — Eighth Smaraṇa)
summary: This is a short hymn of praise to two Tīrthaṅkaras at once — Ajitanātha (the 2nd) and Śāntinātha (the 16th) — recited together for protection and blessing. In eight compact Prakrit-Apabhraṁśa verses it walks through their whole life-story (royal birth, great charity, renunciation, fierce austerity, omniscience, and final liberation) and then promises that whoever holds this pair of Jinas in the heart is freed from fear, affliction, and misfortune. It is the eighth of the Nine Smaraṇas (Nava Smaraṇa), the set of protective stotras recited in the fortnightly Pākṣika pratikramaṇa. It is called 'short' (laghu) to distinguish it from the longer Ajitaśānti hymns in the same collection.
ritualPlacement: It is the 8th of the Nine Smaraṇas (Nava Smaraṇa), a fixed set of nine protective stotras recited within the Pākṣika (Pakkhī, fortnightly) pratikramaṇa, after the Aṣṭottarī Tīrthamālā and the two Nandī-sūtra sajjhāys and before the Pakkhī Khāmaṇā. Because the Cāturmāsika and Sāṁvatsarī pratikramaṇas in this tradition carry no independent text of their own — they are performed exactly as the Pākṣika, substituting the word 'pakkhī' with 'cāumāsī' or 'saṁvatsari' and lengthening the kāyotsarga — this same hymn recurs unchanged in all three. Verse 8 itself names those three occasions (saṁvacchariya / pakkhiya / cāumāsie). This framing is stated once here and is not repeated in the per-verse commentaries.
authorAndSource: Attributed in this book to Vīragaṇin (Kaviवीर Gaṇi / Vīra Gaṇin), whose authorial signature appears in the final verse ('kahai kavivīra gaṇi…'). It is a shorter companion to the longer Ajitaśānti Stava (the 2nd Smaraṇa, by Nandiṣeṇasūri) on which the genre of dual Ajita-and-Śānti praise is modelled. Composed in a Prakrit/Apabhraṁśa idiom. Specific date and redaction details are not attested in the source book.
structureArc: The eight verses move chronologically through the shared career of the two Jinas, then turn to the worshipper. Verse 1 invokes them — born in the Ikṣvāku/Kuru lineage, hymned by their mothers and by the Saudharma gods — and salutes them as bringers of auspiciousness. Verse 2 narrates the birth-bath: the 32 Indras bathe the infants on the crest of Meru (Surigiri) amid the joy of khecaras, men, and gods. Verse 3 covers their reign and unmatched generosity, then their renunciation (dīkṣā) in longing for liberation; the worshipper praises their lotus-feet. Verse 4 recounts the fierce austerity, the endurance of upasargas, the destruction of the four 'ghāti' karmas, and the rise of stainless omniscience — and names them plainly as 'the 2nd and the 16th Jina.' Verse 5 gives their preaching that awoke the three worlds, the burning-away of the remaining (bhavopagrāhī) karmas, and their passage to the city of nirvāṇa. Verses 6–8 are the protective and fruit-bearing close: verse 6 lists the dangers dispelled for one who remembers this pair; verse 7 frames the choice (shun poverty and ill-fortune, desire prosperity and welfare, fear rebirth, want the bliss of liberation — therefore be devoted); verse 8 is the colophon naming the recitation-occasions, the author, and the reward — misfortune departs and every good accrues.
recurringImagery: Three motifs run across the verses and are explained here once. (1) The dual subject: this is a 'twin' hymn — almost every verse praises Ajita and Śānti together (ajia-saṁtī), so singular-sounding praise should be read as addressed to both Jinas jointly; verse 4's 'second and sixteenth Jina' is the key that fixes their identities. (2) The Tīrthaṅkara life-template (pañca-kalyāṇaka-like arc): birth bathed by the Indras on Meru (v.2), renunciation (v.3), kevalajñāna after destroying the ghāti karmas (v.4), and final nirvāṇa after the aghātī/bhavopagrāhī karmas fall away (v.5) — the same five-event scaffold underlying most Jina-stavas. (3) The catalogue of terrors: verse 6's list of enemies, lions, elephants, yoginīs, bhūtas, vetālas, rākṣasīs and ḍākinīs is the conventional 'upasarga' inventory of a protective stotra (rakṣā-mantra), set against the single remedy of remembering the Jina-pair in the heart.
keyThemes: joint devotion to Ajitanātha and Śāntinātha
the Tīrthaṅkara life-arc: birth, renunciation, omniscience, liberation
destruction of the four ghāti karmas and attainment of kevalajñāna
protection from fear, affliction, and malign beings (rakṣā)
worldly and liberative reward (prosperity, saubhāgya, śiva-sukha)
recitation merit across pākṣika, cāturmāsika, and sāṁvatsarī occasions

glossary.definition: A hymn of praise to a Jina or holy being; here a short (laghu) one.
A protective 'remembrance' stotra; this is the 8th of the Nine Smaraṇas recited in the Pākṣika pratikramaṇa.
The 2nd of the 24 Tīrthaṅkaras, one of the two Jinas praised here.
The 16th Tīrthaṅkara, the second of the two Jinas praised here.
A 'ford-maker'; an enlightened teacher who reveals the path of liberation, of whom there are twenty-four in each cosmic age.
Renunciation; formal entry into the ascetic life in pursuit of liberation (v.3).
An affliction or attack — whether by humans, animals, or supernatural beings — endured by an ascetic (v.4) and dispelled for the devotee (v.6).
The four 'destructive' karmas that obscure knowledge, perception, energy, and bring delusion; their destruction yields omniscience (v.4).
Stainless, all-encompassing omniscience attained on the destruction of the ghāti karmas (v.4).
The remaining (aghātī) karmas that sustain embodied existence; their exhaustion precedes final liberation (v.5).
Final liberation, here imaged as the 'city of nirvāṇa' the Jinas reach (v.5).
The cosmic axis-mountain on whose summit the Indras ceremonially bathe a new-born Tīrthaṅkara (v.2).
A celestial king; thirty-two Indras perform the birth-bath of the Jinas (v.2).
A malign spirit/zombie-demon, one of the terrors listed among the upasargas dispelled in verse 6.
A demoness/witch, another of the malign beings whose harm is averted by remembering the Jina-pair (v.6).
Good fortune and well-being; one of the worldly boons promised to the devotee (v.7).
The rite of reflection and atonement within which the Nine Smaraṇas, including this hymn, are recited.

howToRead: A newcomer can read it as a single flowing life-story of two Jinas (verses 1–5) followed by a promise of protection and reward (verses 6–8) — let the per-verse notes supply the detail. An advanced practitioner should attend to the dual address throughout, the precise karma-theory vocabulary of verses 4–5, and verse 8's liturgical placement of the hymn across the pākṣika, cāturmāsika, and sāṁvatsarī observances.

sources.title: Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha edition)
sources.consultedFor: Identity of the hymn as the 8th Smaraṇa, its author Vīragaṇin, and its placement within the Pākṣika pratikramaṇa

notes: The hymn praises Ajitanātha (2nd) and Śāntinātha (16th) jointly, as fixed by verse 4 ('duiya solasama jiṇa'); it is the shorter counterpart to the longer Ajitaśānti Stava (2nd Smaraṇa, by Nandiṣeṇasūri). Authorship is taken from the in-text colophon (verse 8, 'kavivīra gaṇi') and the book's attribution to Vīragaṇin; precise date and redaction are not attested in the source book and are left uncertain. The verse text is in a mixed Prakrit/Apabhraṁśa idiom with the spelling preserved verbatim from the book; transliterations of individual words in commentary should follow that text. The sample source URL is a placeholder for the printed edition and should be replaced with a stable citation when verified.
